text: hi everyone this is dr shinjini and my today's video is a surgical discussion of pelvic lymph node dissection now all of us know that pelvic lymph node dissection is one of the leading surgical procedures in gynecologic oncology practice therefore learning the proper technique with anatomic landmarks will improve surgical skills and confidence
Pelvic lymphadenectomy is a supplementary part of staging and treatment in gynecologic oncology. Also, it influences the prognosis of many tumours and guides the adjuvant treatment.

Now we all know that the role of lymphadenectomy in ovarian cancer is controversial. In endometrial cancer, the high risk patient deserves a pelvic lymphadenectomy all the way up till the pariotic nodes and in cervical cancer, pelvic lymphadenectomy is a complementary part of the surgical treatment.

Now this video of mine will demonstrate the limited pelvic lymphadenectomy which is seen on the left side of the current figure. The extended pelvic lymph node dissection which means that we clear the roots of all the divisions of the internal iliac will not be discussed in this video.

The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks for pelvic lymph node dissection: the genitofemoral nerve laterally, the bifurcation of the common iliac artery cranially, the deep circumflex iliac vein caudally, the obturator nerve inferiorly, and the obliterated umbilical artery medially.
So in this particular video, I have demonstrated bilateral pelvic lymph node dissection using the ultrasonic scalpel only. So let us proceed to the video now.
The surgeon prepares to start the pelvic lymph node dissection following the completion of the hysterectomy, utilizing the ultrasonic harmonic scalpel for the procedure.

The surgeon holds the harmonic scalpel in the right hand while using a Babcock's forceps to gently grasp vessels and lymph nodes without causing damage. Two bowel holding graspers are visible on the assisting ports.

The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision parallel and upwards to the lateral border of the external iliac artery, exposing the underlying psoas muscle. The genitofemoral nerve becomes visible upon exposure of the psoas muscle.
The surgeon moves upwards with strokes, revealing the deep circumflex iliac vein in the surgical field. The vein is marked with two blue lines on the screen for clear identification.

The surgeon identifies a large lymph node, known as Jackson's node, above the upper limit of the pelvic lymph node dissection area. This node is visually prominent but is not removed due to its typical non-positive status and association with leg edema.

The surgeon identifies the upper limit of the deep circumflex iliac vein and begins curving the instrument downwards towards the ilio obturator fossa. The ultrasonic scalpel is used to dissect, revealing the medial border of the external iliac vein.

The surgeon moves into the ilioobturator fossa, revealing the superior ramus of the pubic bone, also known as the pectineal ligament. The bone appears as a wide, glistening window reflecting light, indicating successful dissection.

The surgeon focuses on developing the external iliac vessels, removing the lymph nodes surrounding the external iliac artery and vein. The inter iliac nodes, located between the artery and vein, are also cleared. The vessels are further developed from their posterior aspect, with visual attention on how this is achieved.

The surgeon clears the anterior wall of the external iliac vessels, gently breaking off connections of the fibro-fatty nodal tissue. The harmonic device is used with gentle and small movements to achieve precise dissection at the base of the external iliac vessels.

The surgeon clears the posterior aspect of the external iliac vessels, developing the iliolumbar space between the major vessels and the psoas muscle. The external iliac artery and vein are visibly lifted away from the psoas muscle bed.

The surgeon lifts the external iliac vessels to facilitate the dissection of the ilio obturator space. The bifurcation of the external iliac vein and internal iliac vein is identified, and a large vein is visible, which is not routinely present.

The aberrant obturator vein is visible, draining directly into the external iliac vein. The obturator nerve emerges between the fork of the external iliac vein and the internal iliac vein during the dissection.
The internal iliac vein is visible and marked with blue color, while the obturator nerve is marked with yellow color. These landmarks are crucial for ensuring a safe nodal dissection.

The surgeon follows the obturator nerve, identifying a large vein running above it and draining into the external iliac vein. The assistant uses a grasper to guard the vein while the surgeon clears its superior border with a Babcock's forcep. The vein runs parallel to the obturator nerve, which is visible entering the obturator foramen alongside the vein.

so now let me tell you that abnormalities of obturator vessels the vein and the arteries are one of the common pelvic vascular abnormalities that may be seen and please remember that the obturator artery though can arise from external iliac or the inferior epigastric artery however this kind of aberration or malformation you can say is pretty rare but the aberrant obturator vein is a much more common kind of aberration that you may see and in this particular variant it is arising from the external iliac vein you can see it nice stout and moving towards the obturator foramen Sometimes the aberrant obturator vein may arise or drain into the inferior epigastric vein. This may happen medial to the femoral ring or lateral to the femoral ring as well. So these are the three common variations in the obturator vein when it is known as the aberrant obturator vein.

The surgeon identifies the obturator artery and notes the presence of an aberrant obturator vein draining into the external iliac vein. The lymph node dissection continues following the identification of these vascular structures.

If you do not have knowledge of this aberration or let us say alteration in the anatomy, sometimes it is easy to damage these vessels and if there is a damage then there could be a haemorrhage which could be a slight task to control. Remember if the aberrant obturator vein is damaged near its insertion into the external iliac and if there is a disconnection there then the system may bleed very very heavily.
In the right hemipelvis, the external iliac artery is clearly delineated alongside the external iliac vein. The surgeon is clearing the inter iliac group after lymph node removal on the left side.
The surgeon performs a peritoneal incision parallel and upwards to the external iliac artery, preparing to remove the nodal chain from the external iliac artery.

The surgeon uses small movements with a harmonic scalpel to gently dissect upwards, making small strokes. The dissection continues until the deep circumflex iliac vein is visible, draining into the external iliac vein and crossing over the external iliac artery, marking the caudal limit of the dissection towards the leg end.

The surgeon identifies the cranial limit at the bifurcation of the common iliac artery into the external and internal iliac arteries. A lymph node is visible near this bifurcation, and the deep circumflex iliac vein is observed crossing over the external iliac artery to drain into the external iliac vein.

The surgeon identifies the deep circumflex iliac vein as a landmark and proceeds to develop the medial border of the external iliac vein to gain entry into the ilio-obturator fossa. The superior ramus or pectineal ligament is visible, glistening as the surgeon broadens the surgical windows. Traction is applied to the obliterated umbilical artery.

The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery as the medial landmark in the pelvis. A large vein is observed, indicating a variation in the pelvic anatomy. The surgeon exercises caution, considering the possibility of a duplicated aberrant obturator vein on the opposite side.

The surgeon carefully removes lymph nodes located above and below the deep circumflex iliac vein, ensuring the vein remains undamaged. The nodal mass is slipped from behind the vein, revealing the obturator nerve in the surgical field.

The ilio-obturator lymph nodes have been cleared, revealing the obturator internus muscle and the obturator nerve, which is held firmly to facilitate the removal of the nodal mass. The internal iliac vein is visible below the obturator nerve, serving as a crucial landmark for safe lymph node dissection.

The surgeon identifies the internal iliac vein and confidently maneuvers around the obturator nerve to clear lymph nodes. The internal iliac vein is visible, running alongside the bifurcation of the external and internal iliac venous systems. The medial border near the bifurcation is cleared, with the internal iliac vein consistently in view, allowing for precise removal of the nodal mass.

The obturator vein is visibly draining directly into the external iliac vein and into the obturator foramen. The final view of the lymph node dissection reveals clear anatomical structures, including the obturator vein and surrounding tissues.

The surgeon identifies the deep circumflex iliac vein and the inferior epigastric vein, with the aberrant obturator vein joining into the obturator foramen. The visual field includes these vascular structures, but the corona mortis is not identifiable in this case.

The surgeon completes the lymph node dissection in both fossae using an ultrasonic scalpel, achieving clear visualization of anatomical landmarks such as the obturator nerve and surrounding structures.
